title: Hanging lining paper before decorating
subtitle: Level uneven walls and give paint or wallpaper a stable base
excerpt: How to hang horizontal lining paper on walls before painting or papering. Covers surface preparation, cutting and pasting, correct soaking time, seam placement, and the drying period before the final finish.
sourceNotes: Practical Paperhanging, Leslie Hayward (1978). Collins Complete DIY Manual, Albert Jackson and David Day (1989). British Gypsum White Book (substrate preparation). City and Guilds Level 2 Painting and Decorating syllabi.

glossaryTerms.definition: A plain off-white paper hung on walls or ceilings before the final decoration. Sold in grades by weight: 800 grade for sound surfaces, 1200 grade for uneven walls, 1400 grade for ceilings and problem areas. Hung horizontally (cross-lined) before wallpaper so that seams do not coincide and cause ridges in the top paper.

Hanging lining paper at right angles to the final wallpaper so that neither layer's seams coincide. Coinciding seams cause ridge lines and bubbles in the top paper. Cross-lining is standard practice when lining paper precedes patterned wallpaper.

The period between pasting wallpaper or lining paper and hanging it, during which the paste soaks into the paper and allows it to expand fully. Hanging before the paper has fully expanded means it will continue expanding on the wall, causing bubbles. Typical soaking time: 2 to 3 minutes for lining paper, 5 to 10 minutes for heavier wallpaper.

content.text: is hung before the final decoration to fill minor hollows, cover hairline cracks, and give the wall a uniform porosity. On new or recently patched plaster it reduces suction. On uneven surfaces it masks undulations that paint alone would show. It is hung horizontally on walls;
; so that its seams do not coincide with the seams of any wallpaper that follows. On a ceiling, lining runs across the room at right angles to the main window.

Lining paper (grade 800 for sound walls, 1200 for uneven surfaces). Ready-mixed paste or powder paste mixed to the manufacturer's ratio. A paste bucket and paste brush. A pasting table. A straight-edge or long ruler. A utility knife with fresh blades. A seam roller. A clean sponge. A pencil.

Fill all cracks and dents with a lightweight filler. Once dry, rub the surface smooth with the sanding block. Remove loose or flaking paint. Dust the surface down. On bare new plaster, apply a diluted PVA coat (1 part PVA to 4 parts water) and allow it to cure to a light tack; this prevents paste drying out too fast and the paper lifting at the seams.
Step 2, mark a level starting line

Hold the spirit level horizontally on the wall at the intended height of the first strip; typically the roll width down from the ceiling, less 25 mm to allow the top edge to tuck under the ceiling line. Mark the level line in pencil across the full wall. This keeps the first strip horizontal and prevents the paper from creeping out of level as you work along the wall.

Measure the wall width and add 50 mm for trimming at both ends. Cut the strip on the pasting table. Apply paste evenly from the centre outward to avoid trapping air. Fold the pasted strip accordion-style (pasted face to pasted face) so it can be carried to the wall without stretching. Allow the
Step 4, hang and smooth the strip

Unfold the accordion from the top and press the top edge to the wall, aligning it with the pencil line. Smooth outward from the centre with a clean sponge or paperhanging brush, working air bubbles to the edges. At corners, lap the paper 25 mm around onto the adjacent wall; butt the next strip into the corner on top of the lap. At each end, crease the overlap into the ceiling or skirting angle with the filling knife and trim cleanly with the utility knife.
Step 5, hang subsequent strips and leave to dry

Drop the next strip below the first, butting (not overlapping) the seam. A gap of 1 mm at the seam is better than an overlap, which creates a ridge visible under paint. Run the seam roller gently along the join once the paste has partly set (about 15 minutes). Wipe paste off the face immediately with a damp sponge: dried paste shows as a sheen under emulsion. Allow 24 hours for the lining paper to dry before painting, or 5 to 14 days before hanging wallpaper on top.

attrs.symptom: Seams lifting within hours of hanging
attrs.cause: Paste dried too fast due to high-suction wall or paste too thin.
attrs.fix: Apply a diluted PVA coat to the bare wall, allow to reach a light tack, and re-hang. Use a slightly stiffer paste mix on high-suction surfaces.

attrs.symptom: Bubbles remaining after the paper has dried
attrs.cause: Insufficient soaking time; the paper continued expanding after it was fixed to the wall.
attrs.fix: Allow the full 2 to 3 minutes soaking time for each strip before hanging. Bubbles that appear during drying often flatten as the paper dries completely — leave 24 hours before deciding to re-hang.

attrs.symptom: A ridge visible under paint at every seam
attrs.fix: Light sanding with 120-grit paper reduces the ridge. Fill with a flexible filler if the ridge is pronounced. On new work, butt seams without overlap.
